Chapter 3 · §3.1 · mock-milestone review

Derivatives of Sine and Cosine

19 教學單元 + intro / 4 divider / outro = 25 場景 · Route A（Plex+LaTeX）· 1080p mock · 旁白已 sign-off（LOCKED）

殘留 advisory（3，cosmetic，mock 里程碑保留記錄）：
  • squeeze_graph [V2] cosθ 曲線標籤對比偏低（疊在灰 cosθ 線上、可讀不蓋資訊） — 保留——cosmetic、無教學影響

  • slope_equals_height [V4] y=cos x 指定 dashed 但渲成實線 — 保留——同上；sin/cos 以顏色與標籤可辨、無教學影響
00 intro intro
01 divider_limit divider The Limit Behind the Slope
02 why_trig_is_different definition_math A Limit No Algebra Can Crack
Every derivative so far has yielded to algebra. For a polynomial we expanded $(x+h)^n$ and canceled the factor of $h$; for $e^x$ the power series did the same job. The trigonometric functions are different in kind. Write down the difference quotient for $\sin x$, and the usual move stalls: no identity cancels that $h$ in the denominator, because $\sin(x+h)$ does not split into pieces that subtract. As we are about to see, the whole computation funnels down to a single limit, the limit of $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$ as $\theta\to 0$, which has the indeterminate form zero over zero and which no algebra will crack. Breaking it open takes a genuinely new tool -- a geometric comparison of areas on the unit circle -- and once we secure it, it unlocks the derivatives of both sine and cosine at once. One ground rule first: throughout, angles are measured in radians, and that turns out to be essential.
Clean definition slide: cyan kicker, bold white title with navy spine, prose statement, clear vertical flow from the sin difference quotient to the amber sinθ/θ limit with its (0/0) tag — strong hierarchy, generous margins.
03 difference_quotient_for_sine derivation The Difference Quotient for Sine
So let us transform that difference quotient. The decisive step is a sum-to-product identity, applied with $A=x+h$ and $B=x$. The numerator becomes $2\cos(x+\tfrac h2)\sin\tfrac h2$. Now divide by $h$, writing $h$ as $2$ times $\tfrac h2$ to match the half-angle inside the sine -- the quotient cleans up to $\cos(x+\tfrac h2)$ times $\sin(h/2)$ over $h/2$. Two factors, and as $h\to 0$ each one moves: the cosine factor slides to $\cos x$, provided cosine is continuous, and the second is exactly $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$ with $\theta=\tfrac h2$. So the whole derivative rests on two facts -- continuity, and that fundamental limit. We secure both now.
Clean three-row derivation: [EXAMPLE] eyebrow, two equations with dashed leaders to reason labels, amber-highlighted result row. Strong logic-flow top-to-bottom; all Tex legible.
04 divider_squeeze divider A Geometric Squeeze
05 sector_inequality graph⚙️ custom hook A Squeeze on the Unit Circle
First, one convenience. Replacing $\theta$ by $-\theta$ flips the sign of both $\theta$ and $\sin\theta$, so the ratio $\sin\theta/\theta$ is even -- it is enough to chase $\theta$ down to zero from the positive side. Now the geometry. On the unit circle, $A$ is at $(1,0)$ and $B$ is the point at angle $\theta$; extend the radius to meet the tangent at $A$ in the point $C=(1,\tan\theta)$. The inner triangle $OAB$ has base one and height $\sin\theta$, so its area is one half $\sin\theta$. The sector $OAB$ is the fraction $\theta$ over $2\pi$ of the disc -- area one half $\theta$. And the outer triangle $OAC$ has height $\tan\theta$, so its area is one half $\tan\theta$. Each region sits inside the next, so the areas line up in order: one half $\sin\theta$, then one half $\theta$, then one half $\tan\theta$.
Clean, well-balanced unit-circle squeeze figure: nested inner triangle (teal), sector wedge (orange), outer-triangle height AC (green) color-coded to the ½sinθ ≤ ½θ ≤ ½tanθ chain; geometry faithful (B on circle and radius OC, A=(1,0), C above A).
06 squeeze_to_the_bound derivation Trapping the Ratio
Now turn that chain of areas into a statement about $\sin\theta/\theta$. Multiply through by $2$: $\sin\theta\le\theta\le\tan\theta$. Divide by $\sin\theta$, positive here, and rewrite $\tan\theta$ as $\sin\theta$ over $\cos\theta$ -- giving $1$ at most $\theta$ over $\sin\theta$ at most $1$ over $\cos\theta$. Every term is positive, so taking reciprocals reverses the inequalities: $\cos\theta\le\sin\theta/\theta\le 1$, for $\theta$ between zero and $\tfrac\pi2$. And tuck one more in your pocket -- from $\sin\theta\le \theta$ we get zero at most $|\sin\theta|$ at most $|\theta|$ near zero, a bound we lean on twice in a moment.
Clean derivation: four left-aligned inequality rows with dashed leaders to reasons, orange highlight on the key result line; Tex legible.
07 continuity_of_sin_cos theorem_proof Sine and Cosine Are Continuous
That little bound does more than help with the limit -- it is what makes sine and cosine continuous, exactly what those two factors needed. Here is the claim: $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ are continuous at every real $x_0$. Since zero at most $|\sin\theta|$ at most $|\theta|$, the squeeze theorem forces $\sin\theta\to 0$. And the sum-to-product identities bound both changes by at most $2|\sin\tfrac{x-x_0}{2}|$, which goes to zero as $x\to x_0$. Squeeze once more: $\cos x\to\cos x_0$ and $\sin x\to\sin x_0$ -- both continuous.
Clean theorem_proof: [PROPOSITION] eyebrow, accent-bordered statement box, two proof lines, green QED marker. Tex matches storyboard verbatim; good vertical rhythm.
08 fundamental_limit theorem_proof The Fundamental Limit
Now we can finish the limit that started all this. The claim is clean: $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$ tends to one as $\theta\to 0$. For $\theta$ between zero and $\tfrac\pi2$, $\cos\theta\le \sin\theta/\theta\le 1$. The floor $\cos\theta$ tends to one by continuity, the ceiling is one -- so squeeze pins the ratio to one from the right. And it is even, so the two-sided limit is one. That is the keystone.
Clean theorem-proof: boxed statement, two-step proof with cyan bullets, green QED with tombstone glyph; navy-spine left margin, no crowding.
09 squeeze_graph graph The Squeeze, Drawn
It helps to see that squeeze as a picture. Here runs $\cos\theta$, the dashed floor. And the ceiling -- the constant line at height one. Between them, the ratio $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$, caught in the narrowing gap. As $\theta$ slides toward zero the floor rises to one and the ceiling already is one, so the curve is squeezed right to one. Notice the open circle at $\theta=0$: the ratio is undefined there, zero over zero. So what equals one is the limit -- the height the curve is forced toward, not a value it ever takes.
Clean squeeze diagram: orange ratio trapped between cyan y=1 ceiling and muted cosθ floor, converging on the hollow open circle at (0,1).
10 limit_not_identity callout A Limit, Not an Identity
One caution, because this is easy to over-read. The statement is about the limit as $\theta\to 0$ -- not an identity. At a fixed angle the ratio is generally not one: at $\theta=\tfrac\pi2$ it is two over $\pi$, about $0.64$, and at $\theta=\pi$ it is zero. The value one is reached only in the limit.
Clean CAUTION callout: navy card with orange spine + warning icon; body and all math (θ→0, sinθ/θ, π/2, 2/π≈0.64, θ=π) render crisply.
11 radians_essential callout Why Radians
A second caution, and this one is structural. Everything here depends on radians. The sector area one half $\theta$ -- and with it the limit -- uses radian measure. Switch to degrees and the limit is $\pi$ over $180$, not one, so the derivative drags that factor along. The clean formulas $\sin'=\cos$ and $\cos'=-\sin$ hold only in radians.
Clean CAUTION callout: red accent bar + icon; three teaching equations (degree limit = π/180, dragged derivative, clean radian sin'=cos) in clear top-to-bottom flow.
12 divider_derivatives divider The Two Derivatives
13 derivative_of_sine theorem_proof The Derivative of Sine
Now the payoff. With continuity and the fundamental limit in hand, the difference quotient for sine all but evaluates itself. The claim: the derivative of $\sin x$ is $\cos x$, for every real $x$. We rewrote the quotient as $\cos(x+\tfrac h2)$ times $\sin(h/2)$ over $h/2$. Let $h\to 0$: the first factor tends to $\cos x$ by continuity, the second to one by the fundamental limit. Their product tends to $\cos x$ times one -- just $\cos x$.
Clean theorem-proof card: boxed statement, two bulleted proof steps, green QED line; left-aligned, strong hierarchy and Tex legibility.
14 derivative_of_cosine theorem_proof The Derivative of Cosine
Cosine falls to the very same machinery -- only the identity changes. The claim: the derivative of $\cos x$ is $-\sin x$. Using the companion identity, the difference quotient becomes $-\sin(x+\tfrac h2)$ times $\sin(h/2)$ over $h/2$. Send $h$ to zero: the sine factor goes to $\sin x$ by continuity, the other to one. What is left is $-\sin x$. Same two tools, one extra minus sign.
Clean theorem-proof: accent-bar title with THEOREM badge, boxed statement, spaced PROOF list ending in green QED; Tex legible, comfortable margin.
15 slope_equals_height graph⚙️ custom hook Slope Equals Height
Here is what that theorem looks like on the graph. Draw $y=\sin x$, and check the slope of its tangent at a few points. At $x=0$ it is climbing at slope one. At $x=\tfrac\pi2$, the top of the hump, the tangent is flat -- slope zero. At $x=\pi$ it is falling at slope minus one. Now lay $y=\cos x$ on top, and read its heights at those same points: one, zero, minus one. They match exactly -- the slope of sine is the height of cosine, the theorem drawn.
Clean layout: orange sin vs gray cos read clearly, three green tangents (m=1,0,-1) align with the orange cos-height dots (1,0,-1); labels well-separated, no collisions. (Nit: cos dashing spec-vs-render mismatch, non-blocking.)
16 derivative_cycle definition_math The Derivative Cycle
Step back and watch the pattern. Differentiation sends sine and cosine into each other, with a minus sign that turns over every second step. Writing an arrow for one derivative: $\sin x$ to $\cos x$ to $-\sin x$ to $-\cos x$, and back to $\sin x$. Four derivatives, and you are home -- the fourth derivative of sine is sine again. Where $e^x$ reproduces itself in one step, these do it in a cycle of four: the self-renewal that lets them oscillate forever without winding down.
Clean definition_math: clear hierarchy (eyebrow / title / statement / two amber equations); cycle chain and d⁴/dx⁴ identity render crisply, match storyboard.
17 divider_apply divider Consequences and Applications
18 companion_limit derivation A Companion Limit
The fundamental limit has a companion worth knowing: $(1-\cos\theta)$ over $\theta$ tends to zero. It is again zero over zero -- so multiply top and bottom by $1+\cos\theta$. The numerator becomes $\sin^2\theta$, and the whole thing splits into $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$ times $\sin\theta$ over $1+\cos\theta$. Let $\theta\to 0$: the first factor goes to one, the second to zero over two. One times zero is zero.
Clean three-row derivation: left-aligned equations with dashed leaders to reasons, amber accent on the final limit result; strong hierarchy, uncrowded.
19 all_six_trig_derivatives derivation All Six Derivatives
With sine and cosine handled, the other four cost almost nothing -- just the quotient rule. Take $\tan x=\sin x$ over $\cos x$. The quotient rule gives this. The numerator is $\cos^2 x+\sin^2 x$, which is one -- so the derivative of $\tan x$ is $\sec^2 x$. Secant is the same idea on $1$ over $\cos x$: its derivative is $\sec x\tan x$. And the same move on $\cot x$ and $\csc x$ finishes all six -- every one riding on $\sin'=\cos$ and $\cos'=-\sin$.
Clean derivation: consistent left-aligned math rows with dashed leaders to reasons; highlighted gold sec-result row. (Garbled reason fixed → now reads SECANT, SAME RULE.)
20 shm_compute derivation A Weight on a Spring
Let us put the new derivatives to work on something moving. A weight on a spring has height $s(t)=\sin t$. Velocity is its derivative, $s'(t)=\cos t$. Acceleration is the next derivative, $s''(t)=-\sin t$. But $-\sin t$ is just $-s(t)$ -- at every instant the weight is pushed back toward rest. That relation, $s''=-s$, is the signature of simple harmonic motion; sine and cosine are exactly the functions that describe it.
Clean derivation: four equation rows with dashed leaders, SHM result row highlighted amber to mark the punchline; strong hierarchy, ample whitespace.
21 shm_stacked_graphs graph⚙️ custom hook Height, Velocity, Acceleration
Stack the three graphs over one time axis and the relationship jumps out. On top, the height $s=\sin t$. In the middle, the velocity $s'=\cos t$. On the bottom, the acceleration $s''=-\sin t$. Watch where the height hits a peak or a trough -- there the velocity passes through zero, because at the extremes the weight momentarily stops. And hold the bottom graph against the top: it is the top one flipped upside down. That is $s''=-s$, drawn -- the very picture of an oscillation.
Clean three-row stack with color coding (orange sin / blue cos / green −sin), aligned baselines, shared dashed guides at t=π/2, 3π/2 tying peaks-to-zeros. (Mild left-band whitespace, within house style.)
22 toward_the_chain_rule definition_math Toward the Chain Rule
We can now differentiate every trigonometric function -- but only in its bare form. We can handle $\sin x$, yet not $\sin(x^2)$ or $\sin(3x+1)$, where the angle is itself a function tucked inside. To reach a function buried inside another, we need one more rule -- a way to differentiate a composition. That is the chain rule, and it is what comes next; with it, every derivative we found today becomes the seed of a whole family more.
Clean definition slide: [DEFINITION] eyebrow + title, two-line statement, one highlighted math row (sin x ✓, sin(x²)?, sin(3x+1)?). Forward-reference closing slide.
23 recap recap_cards Key Takeaways
Pull the section together. Everything grew from one limit: $\sin\theta$ over $\theta$ tends to one, cracked by squeezing on the unit circle. That limit, with continuity, gave the two derivatives at the heart of the section -- $\sin'=\cos$ and $\cos'=-\sin$. From those two, the quotient rule delivered all the other trig derivatives. And keep the cycle in mind -- four derivatives bring you home -- with the fine print that it all depends on radians. Hold the keystone close. And the two derivatives that follow from it.
Clean recap card: navy spine + header, four numbered takeaways left, two boxed REMEMBER formulas right; all Tex crisp, good hierarchy.
24 outro outro
